title: Chinese Models And The US AI Boom
gist: Gavekal's Will Denyer argues that Z.ai's GLM-5.2 model—which nearly matches Anthropic and OpenAI while outperforming Google—and Microsoft's consideration of adding DeepSeek to Copilot confirm Chinese AI is now a front-line competitor with 'far reaching' implications for US and global equities.

insights.claim: Z.ai's GLM-5.2 model almost matches the latest models from Anthropic and OpenAI, and outperforms Google's top model—described as the most impressive Chinese challenge yet to US dominance in artificial intelligence.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Chinese AI has crossed a performance threshold that makes it a credible substitute for US frontier models; companies and investors treating Chinese AI as inferior face a structural mispricing risk.
insights.quote: the new model from Beijing-based and Hong Kong-listed Z.ai (formerly Zhipu AI) almost matches the latest models from Anthropic and OpenAI, and outperforms Google's top model
insights.locator: With the launch of its GLM-5.2 model

insights.claim: Microsoft is considering adding models from China's DeepSeek to its Copilot offerings, betting that many consumers will favor 'cheap good-enough' models over more expensive leading-edge products.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: If Microsoft integrates DeepSeek into Copilot, it validates the 'good-enough AI' commoditization thesis and materially threatens the premium pricing power of US frontier model providers like OpenAI and Anthropic.
insights.quote: Microsoft is considering adding models from China's DeepSeek to its Copilot offerings. If it does, it will be betting that many consumers will favor cheap good-enough models over more expensive leading-edge products.
insights.locator: At the same time, Microsoft is considering

insights.claim: Together the GLM-5.2 launch and potential Microsoft/DeepSeek integration confirm that Chinese AI is now a 'front-line competitor' with implications for US and global equities that Gavekal describes as 'far reaching'.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: The investment implications span multiple equity groups: AI hardware suppliers (demand destruction from cheaper models), US software vendors (pricing pressure), and Chinese tech firms (rerating higher); Gavekal's framing suggests these are non-trivial portfolio reallocations.
insights.quote: Together, these two developments confirm that Chinese AI is now a front-line competitor. The implications for US and global equities are far reaching.
